revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''雑書'''（ざっしょ）とは、[[近代]]以前における各種の[[暦]]・[[占]]に関する書物の総称を指す。 [[暦注]]などに記載さ...'
revision.text:
'''雑書'''（ざっしょ）とは、[[近代]]以前における各種の[[暦]]・[[占]]に関する書物の総称を指す。
[[暦注]]などに記載された[[八卦]]・[[方位]]・[[干支]]・[[納音]]・[[十二直]]・[[星宿]]・[[七曜]]などに記載された吉凶や様々な[[禁忌]]をはじめとする各種暦占の解説が主である。
『[[本朝書籍目録]]』には[[平安時代]]に[[賀茂家栄]]が『雑書』（『新書』とする伝本もある）を著したと伝えるが、現存最古のものは[[室町時代]]のものである。[[江戸時代]]まで日常生活から男女関係までの各種分野を扱った多数の雑書が刊行されているが、対象となる読者層に合わせる形で、[[漢文]]・[[和文]]の文体や[[具注暦]]・[[仮名暦]]の解説などの違いがあった。
== 参考文献 == *岡田芳朗「雑書」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:さつしよ}} [[Category:暦注]]

{{独自研究}}'''雑書'''（ざっしょ）とは、[[近代]]以前における各種の[[暦]]・[[占]]に関する書物の総称を指す。
[[暦注]]などに記載された[[八卦]]・[[方位]]・[[干支]]・[[納音]]・[[十二直]]・[[星宿]]・[[七曜]]などに記載された吉凶や様々な[[禁忌]]をはじめとする各種暦占の解説が主である。
『[[本朝書籍目録]]』には[[平安時代]]に[[賀茂家栄]]が『雑書』（『新書』とする伝本もある）を著したと伝えるが、現存最古のものは[[室町時代]]のものである。[[江戸時代]]まで日常生活から男女関係までの各種分野を扱った多数の雑書が刊行されているが、対象となる読者層に合わせる形で、[[漢文]]・[[和文]]の文体や[[具注暦]]・[[仮名暦]]の解説などの違いがあった。
== 参考文献 == *岡田芳朗「雑書」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:さつしよ}} [[Category:暦注]]

{{独自研究}} '''雑書'''（ざっしょ）とは、[[近代]]以前における各種の[[暦]]・[[占]]に関する書物の総称を指す。
[[暦注]]などに記載された[[八卦]]・[[方位]]・[[干支]]・[[納音]]・[[十二直]]・[[星宿]]・[[七曜]]などに記載された吉凶や様々な[[禁忌]]をはじめとする各種暦占の解説が主である。[[陰陽道]]の書物の影響を強く受けて発達したと考えられている。
『[[本朝書籍目録]]』には[[平安時代]]に[[賀茂家栄]]が『雑書』（『新書』とする伝本もある）を著したと伝えるが、現存最古のものは[[室町時代]]のものである。『[[日葡辞書]]』には「神官の与える厄日と吉日の書いてあるもの」と定義されている。[[江戸時代]]に入ると、'''大雑書'''（おおざっしょ）とも呼ばれる[[和文]]による大型本が刊行されるようになる。[[寛永]]9年（[[1632年]]）の年号が入っている『寛永九年版大ざつしよ』（[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）は大雑書の中でも最古のものである。また、[[元禄]]年間頃から仏教の因果説に由来する[[三世相]]がほとんどの雑書に掲載されるようになった。寛永年間から雑書の刊行が盛んに行われるようになっていったが、対象となる読者層に合わせる形で、[[漢文]]・和文の文体や[[具注暦]]・[[仮名暦]]の解説などの違いがあった。特に[[貞享]]から元禄年間にかけては様々な分野の項目についての記事が増補されるようになり、日常生活から男女関係までの各種分野を扱った多数の雑書が刊行され、最盛期であった[[幕末]]には100種類以上が刊行された。この時期になると、雑書は暦占書としてだけではなく、[[百科事典]]的な役割を果たすようになっていった。
== 参考文献 == *岡田芳朗「雑書」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7） *日本史広辞典編集委員会 編『日本史広辞典』（山川出版社、1997年） ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0 *宮内貴久「雑書」（『日本民俗大辞典 上』（吉川弘文館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1
{{DEFAULTSORT:さつしよ}} [[Category:暦注]]

'''雑書'''（ざっしょ）とは、[[近代]]以前における各種の[[暦]]・[[占]]に関する書物の総称を指す。
[[暦注]]などに記載された[[八卦]]・[[方位]]・[[干支]]・[[納音]]・[[十二直]]・[[星宿]]・[[七曜]]などに記載された吉凶や様々な[[禁忌]]をはじめとする各種暦占の解説が主である。[[陰陽道]]の書物の影響を強く受けて発達したと考えられている。
『[[本朝書籍目録]]』には[[平安時代]]に[[賀茂家栄]]が『雑書』（『新書』とする伝本もある）を著したと伝えるが、現存最古のものは[[室町時代]]のものである。『[[日葡辞書]]』には「神官の与える厄日と吉日の書いてあるもの」と定義されている。[[江戸時代]]に入ると、'''大雑書'''（おおざっしょ）とも呼ばれる[[和文]]による大型本が刊行されるようになる。[[寛永]]9年（[[1632年]]）の年号が入っている『寛永九年版大ざつしよ』（[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）は大雑書の中でも最古のものである。また、[[元禄]]年間頃から仏教の因果説に由来する[[三世相]]がほとんどの雑書に掲載されるようになった。寛永年間から雑書の刊行が盛んに行われるようになっていったが、対象となる読者層に合わせる形で、[[漢文]]・和文の文体や[[具注暦]]・[[仮名暦]]の解説などの違いがあった。特に[[貞享]]から元禄年間にかけては様々な分野の項目についての記事が増補されるようになり、日常生活から男女関係までの各種分野を扱った多数の雑書が刊行され、最盛期であった[[幕末]]には100種類以上が刊行された。この時期になると、雑書は暦占書としてだけではなく、[[百科事典]]的な役割を果たすようになっていった。
== 参考文献 == *岡田芳朗「雑書」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7） *日本史広辞典編集委員会 編『日本史広辞典』（山川出版社、1997年） ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0 *宮内貴久「雑書」（『日本民俗大辞典 上』（吉川弘文館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1
{{DEFAULTSORT:さつしよ}} [[Category:暦注]]

revision.comment: 「天保新選永代大雑書万暦大成」の情報を追加。
revision.text:
'''雑書'''（ざっしょ）とは、[[近代]]以前における各種の[[暦]]・[[占]]に関する書物の総称を指す。
[[暦注]]などに記載された[[八卦]]・[[方位]]・[[干支]]・[[納音]]・[[十二直]]・[[星宿]]・[[七曜]]などに記載された吉凶や様々な[[禁忌]]をはじめとする各種暦占の解説が主である。[[陰陽道]]の書物の影響を強く受けて発達したと考えられている。
『[[本朝書籍目録]]』には[[平安時代]]に[[賀茂家栄]]が『雑書』（『新書』とする伝本もある）を著したと伝えるが、現存最古のものは[[室町時代]]のものである。『[[日葡辞書]]』には「神官の与える厄日と吉日の書いてあるもの」と定義されている。[[江戸時代]]に入ると、'''大雑書'''（おおざっしょ）とも呼ばれる[[和文]]による大型本が刊行されるようになる。[[寛永]]9年（[[1632年]]）の年号が入っている『寛永九年版大ざつしよ』（[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）は大雑書の中でも最古のものである。また、[[元禄]]年間頃から仏教の因果説に由来する[[三世相]]がほとんどの雑書に掲載されるようになった。寛永年間から雑書の刊行が盛んに行われるようになっていったが、対象となる読者層に合わせる形で、[[漢文]]・和文の文体や[[具注暦]]・[[仮名暦]]の解説などの違いがあった。特に[[貞享]]から元禄年間にかけては様々な分野の項目についての記事が増補されるようになり、日常生活から男女関係までの各種分野を扱った多数の雑書が刊行され、最盛期であった[[幕末]]には100種類以上が刊行された。この時期になると、雑書は暦占書としてだけではなく、[[百科事典]]的な役割を果たすようになっていった。
『天保新選永代大雑書万暦大成』は[[翻刻]]されたものが出版されている。
== 参考文献 == *岡田芳朗「雑書」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7） *日本史広辞典編集委員会 編『日本史広辞典』（山川出版社、1997年） ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0 *宮内貴久「雑書」（『日本民俗大辞典 上』（吉川弘文館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1 *神宮館「永代大雑書萬暦大成」（『運勢叢書』（神宮館、1998年） ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0）
{{DEFAULTSORT:さつしよ}} [[Category:暦注]]

revision.comment: Created page with "{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times. The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜}}, deta..."
revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
[[Category:Calendrical annotations]]

{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.

{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.

The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜|12=draft|12=draft}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|12=draft|12=draft}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|12=draft|12=draft}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=draft|12=draft}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=draft|12=draft}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書|12=draft|12=draft}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=draft|12=draft}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館|12=draft|12=draft}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄|12=draft|12=draft}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=draft|12=draft}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文|12=draft|12=draft}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享|12=draft|12=draft}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末|12=draft|12=draft}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

revision.comment: 7 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:雑書 up to rev 99333250
revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=2017年8月}} '''雑書'''（ざっしょ）とは、[[近代]]以前における各種の[[暦]]・[[占]]に関する書物の総称を指す。
[[暦注]]などに記載された[[八卦]]・[[方位]]・[[干支]]・[[納音]]・[[十二直]]・[[星宿]]・[[七曜]]などに記載された吉凶や様々な[[タブー|禁忌]]をはじめとする各種暦占の解説が主である。[[陰陽道]]の書物の影響を強く受けて発達したと考えられている。
『[[本朝書籍目録]]』には[[平安時代]]に[[賀茂家栄]]が『雑書』（『新書』とする伝本もある）を著したと伝えるが、現存最古のものは[[室町時代]]のものである。『[[日葡辞書]]』には「神官の与える厄日と吉日の書いてあるもの」と定義されている。[[江戸時代]]に入ると、'''大雑書'''（おおざっしょ）とも呼ばれる[[和文]]による大型本が刊行されるようになる。[[寛永]]9年（[[1632年]]）の年号が入っている『寛永九年版大ざつしよ』（[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）は大雑書の中でも最古のものである。また、[[元禄]]年間頃から仏教の因果説に由来する[[三世相]]がほとんどの雑書に掲載されるようになった。寛永年間から雑書の刊行が盛んに行われるようになっていったが、対象となる読者層に合わせる形で、[[漢文]]・和文の文体や[[具注暦]]・[[仮名暦]]の解説などの違いがあった。特に[[貞享]]から元禄年間にかけては様々な分野の項目についての記事が増補されるようになり、日常生活から男女関係までの各種分野を扱った多数の雑書が刊行され、最盛期であった[[幕末]]には100種類以上が刊行された。この時期になると、雑書は暦占書としてだけではなく、[[百科事典]]的な役割を果たすようになっていった。
『天保新選永代大雑書万暦大成』は[[翻刻]]されたものが出版されている。
== 参考文献 == *岡田芳朗「雑書」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7） *日本史広辞典編集委員会 編『日本史広辞典』（山川出版社、1997年） ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0 *宮内貴久「雑書」（『日本民俗大辞典 上』（吉川弘文館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1 *神宮館「永代大雑書萬暦大成」（『運勢叢書』（神宮館、1998年） ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0）
{{DEFAULTSORT:さつしよ}} [[Category:暦注]]

{{No footnotes|date=2017年8月}} '''雑書'''（ざっしょ）とは、[[近代]]以前における各種の[[暦]]・[[占]]に関する書物の総称を指す。
[[暦注]]などに記載された[[八卦]]・[[方位]]・[[干支]]・[[納音]]・[[十二直]]・[[星宿]]・[[七曜]]などに記載された吉凶や様々な[[タブー|禁忌]]をはじめとする各種暦占の解説が主である。[[陰陽道]]の書物の影響を強く受けて発達したと考えられている。
『[[本朝書籍目録]]』には[[平安時代]]に[[賀茂家栄]]が『雑書』（『新書』とする伝本もある）を著したと伝えるが、現存最古のものは[[室町時代]]のものである。『[[日葡辞書]]』には「神官の与える厄日と吉日の書いてあるもの」と定義されている。[[江戸時代]]に入ると、'''大雑書'''（おおざっしょ）とも呼ばれる[[和文]]による大型本が刊行されるようになる。[[寛永]]9年（[[1632年]]）の年号が入っている『寛永九年版大ざつしよ』（[[国立国会図書館]]蔵）は大雑書の中でも最古のものである。また、[[元禄]]年間頃から仏教の因果説に由来する[[三世相]]がほとんどの雑書に掲載されるようになった。寛永年間から雑書の刊行が盛んに行われるようになっていったが、対象となる読者層に合わせる形で、[[漢文]]・和文の文体や[[具注暦]]・[[仮名暦]]の解説などの違いがあった。特に[[貞享]]から元禄年間にかけては様々な分野の項目についての記事が増補されるようになり、日常生活から男女関係までの各種分野を扱った多数の雑書が刊行され、最盛期であった[[幕末]]には100種類以上が刊行された。この時期になると、雑書は暦占書としてだけではなく、[[百科事典]]的な役割を果たすようになっていった。
『天保新選永代大雑書万暦大成』は[[翻刻]]されたものが出版されている。
== 参考文献 == *岡田芳朗「雑書」（『国史大辞典 6』（吉川弘文館、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7） *日本史広辞典編集委員会 編『日本史広辞典』（山川出版社、1997年） ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0 *宮内貴久「雑書」（『日本民俗大辞典 上』（吉川弘文館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1 *神宮館「永代大雑書萬暦大成」（『運勢叢書』（神宮館、1998年） ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0）

Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文|qq=draft}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|qq=draft}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft}}, catered to specific audiences. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

<!--Miscellaneous works--><!--Miscellaneous works--><!--Miscellaneous works--><!--Miscellaneous works--><!--Miscellaneous works-->

{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜|qq=draft}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|qq=draft}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|qq=draft}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|qq=draft}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書|qq=draft}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|qq=draft}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館|qq=draft}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄|qq=draft}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|qq=draft}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文|qq=draft}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|qq=draft}} and {{ill|kanareki|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享|qq=draft}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末|qq=draft}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
{{translated page|ja|雑書|version=99333250|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Hakke|ja|八卦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakke|qq=}}, {{ill|Hōi|ja|方位|1=Cardinal direction|lt=Five directions|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōi|qq=}}, {{ill|Kanshi|ja|干支|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshi|qq=}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|qq=}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|qq=}}, {{ill|Seishuku|ja|星宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seishuku|qq=}}, and {{ill|Shichiyō|ja|七曜|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichiyō|qq=}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onmyōdō|qq=}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|qq=}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|qq=}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nippo Jisho|qq=}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|qq=}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|ja|国立国会図書館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Diet Library|qq=}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku period|ja|元禄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genroku period|qq=}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|qq=}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|kanbun|ja|漢文|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanbun|qq=}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō era|ja|貞享|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyō era|qq=}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu period|ja|幕末|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bakumatsu period|qq=}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
{{translated page|ja|雑書|version=99333250|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as [[:en:Bagua|Hakke]], [[:en:Cardinal direction|Five directions]], [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Kanshi]], {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|qq=}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|qq=}}, [[:en:Star (Chinese constellation)|Seishuku]], and [[:en:Classical planet|Shichiyō]], detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]] writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|qq=}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|qq=}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|qq=}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|qq=}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
{{translated page|ja|雑書|version=99333250|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Calendrical annotations
revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[calendar|calendrical]] and [[divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as [[:en:Bagua|Hakke]], [[:en:Cardinal direction|Five directions]], [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Kanshi]], {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|qq=}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|qq=}}, [[:en:Star (Chinese constellation)|Seishuku]], and [[:en:Classical planet|Shichiyō]], detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]] writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|qq=}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|qq=}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
{{translated page|ja|雑書|version=99333250|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11659402]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[:en:Calendar|calendrical]] and [[:en:Divination|Divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as [[:en:Bagua|Hakke]], [[:en:Cardinal direction|Five directions]], [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Kanshi]], {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|qq=|1=natchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10465180}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|qq=|1=Twelve Directions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404810}}, [[:en:Star (Chinese constellation)|Seishuku]], and [[:en:Classical planet|Shichiyō]], detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]] writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|qq=|1=Honchō shoseki mokuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|qq=|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659402]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[:en:Calendar|calendrical]] and [[:en:Divination|Divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as [[:en:Bagua|Hakke]], [[:en:Cardinal direction|Five directions]], [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Kanshi]], {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|1=natchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10465180}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|1=Twelve Directions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404810}}, [[:en:Star (Chinese constellation)|Seishuku]], and [[:en:Classical planet|Shichiyō]], detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]] writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Honchō shoseki mokuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
{{translated page|ja|雑書|version=99333250|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659402]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[:en:Calendar|calendrical]] and [[:en:Divination|Divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as [[:en:Bagua|Hakke]], [[:en:Cardinal direction|Five directions]], [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Kanshi]], {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|1=natchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10465180}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|1=Twelve Directions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404810}}, [[:en:Star (Chinese constellation)|Seishuku]], and [[:en:Classical planet|Shichiyō]], detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]] writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Honchō shoseki mokuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Honchō shoseki mokuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659402]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning [[:en:Calendar|calendrical]] and [[:en:Divination|Divination]] matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as [[:en:Bagua|Hakke]], [[:en:Cardinal direction|Five directions]], [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Kanshi]], {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|1=natchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10465180}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|1=Twelve Directions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404810}}, [[:en:Star (Chinese constellation)|Seishuku]], and [[:en:Classical planet|Shichiyō]], detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]] writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Honchō shoseki mokuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Honchō shoseki mokuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.

From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as [[:en:Bagua|Hakke]], [[:en:Cardinal direction|Five directions]], [[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Kanshi]], {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|1=natchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10465180}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|1=Twelve Directions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404810}}, [[:en:Star (Chinese constellation)|Seishuku]], and [[:en:Classical planet|Shichiyō]], detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of [[:en:Onmyōdō|Onmyōdō]] writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Honchō shoseki mokuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. The [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the [[:en:National Diet Library|National Diet Library]].
From the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]] onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as [[:en:Classical Chinese|kanbun]] or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the [[:en:Jōkyō|Jōkyō era]] and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the [[:en:Bakumatsu|Bakumatsu period]], with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11659402]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|WD=Q12132|lt=calendrical}} and {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|WD=Q1043197|lt=Divination}} matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Bagua|en|Bagua|WD=Q340846|lt=Hakke}}, {{ill|Cardinal direction|en|Cardinal direction|WD=Q23718|lt=Five directions}}, {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Kanshi}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|1=natchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10465180}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|1=Twelve Directions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404810}}, {{ill|Star (Chinese constellation)|en|Star (Chinese constellation)|WD=Q1193154|lt=Seishuku}}, and {{ill|Classical planet|en|Classical planet|WD=Q1135397|lt=Shichiyō}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Honchō shoseki mokuroku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|en|Nippo Jisho|WD=Q723637|lt=Nippo Jisho}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=kanbun}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō|en|Jōkyō|WD=Q1146221|lt=Jōkyō era}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu period}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
{{translated page|ja|雑書|version=99333250|comment=Imported full ja history}}

However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|en|Nippo Jisho|WD=Q723637|lt=Nippo Jisho}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=kanbun}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō|en|Jōkyō|WD=Q1146221|lt=Jōkyō era}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu period}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
{{translated page|ja|雑書|version=99333250|comment=Imported full ja history}}
