<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雑書]]
{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|WD=Q12132|lt=calendrical|1=Calendar}} and {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|WD=Q1043197|lt=Divination|1=Divination}} matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Bagua|en|Bagua|WD=Q340846|lt=Hakke|1=Bagua}}, {{ill|Cardinal direction|en|Cardinal direction|WD=Q23718|lt=Five directions|1=Cardinal direction}}, {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Kanshi|1=Sexagenary cycle}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|1=natchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10465180}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|1=Twelve Directions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11404810}}, {{ill|Star (Chinese constellation)|en|Star (Chinese constellation)|WD=Q1193154|lt=Seishuku|1=Star (Chinese constellation)}}, and {{ill|Classical planet|en|Classical planet|WD=Q1135397|lt=Shichiyō|1=Classical planet}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|WD=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Q30923031|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|en|Nippo Jisho|WD=Q723637|lt=Nippo Jisho|1=Nippo Jisho}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|WD=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library|1=National Diet Library}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period|1=Genroku}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|WD=Q37041|lt=kanbun|1=Classical Chinese}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō|en|Jōkyō|WD=Q1146221|lt=Jōkyō era|1=Jōkyō}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|WD=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu period|1=Bakumatsu}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.

revision.text:
{{No footnotes|date=August 2017}} '''Zassho''' (雑書) refers to a general term for various books concerning {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|qid=Q12132|lt=calendrical|1=Calendar}} and {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|qid=Q1043197|lt=Divination|1=Divination}} matters in pre-modern times.
The content primarily included explanations of calendrical and divinatory elements such as {{ill|Bagua|en|Bagua|qid=Q340846|lt=Hakke|1=Bagua}}, {{ill|Cardinal direction|en|Cardinal direction|qid=Q23718|lt=Five directions|1=Cardinal direction}}, {{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|qid=Q246658|lt=Kanshi|1=Sexagenary cycle}}, {{ill|Nattō|ja|納音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nattō|1=natchin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10465180}}, {{ill|Jūni Choku|ja|十二直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūni Choku|1=Twelve Directions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11404810}}, {{ill|Star (Chinese constellation)|en|Star (Chinese constellation)|qid=Q1193154|lt=Seishuku|1=Star (Chinese constellation)}}, and {{ill|Classical planet|en|Classical planet|qid=Q1135397|lt=Shichiyō|1=Classical planet}}, detailing auspicious or inauspicious days and various {{ill|taboo|ja|禁忌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/taboo|qq=}}. These texts are believed to have developed under the strong influence of {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|qid=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}} writings.
According to the {{ill|Honchō shoseki mokuroku|ja|本朝書籍目録|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honchō shoseki mokuroku|1=Q30923031|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30923031}}, a work titled *Zassho* (or *Shinsho* in some versions) was written during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} by {{ill|Kamo no Ienao|ja|賀茂家栄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Ienao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, the oldest surviving examples date back to the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. The {{ill|Nippo Jisho|en|Nippo Jisho|qid=Q723637|lt=Nippo Jisho|1=Nippo Jisho}} defines it as "a book given by Shinto priests containing information on unlucky and auspicious days." During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, large-format books written in {{ill|Japanese|ja|和文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese|1=wabun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11417260}} known as *Ōzassho* (大雑書) began to be published. The oldest example, the *Kan'ei 9 Edition Ōzassho* (1632), is held by the {{ill|National Diet Library|en|National Diet Library|qid=Q477675|lt=National Diet Library|1=National Diet Library}}.
From the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|qid=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period|1=Genroku}} onwards, most Zassho included {{ill|Sansetsu|ja|三世相|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sansetsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, derived from Buddhist causality theories. Beginning in the Kan'ei era, the publication of Zassho became widespread, targeting a broad readership. Differences in writing style, such as {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|qid=Q37041|lt=kanbun|1=Classical Chinese}} or Japanese scripts, and the inclusion of explanations for {{ill|guchūreki|ja|具注暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/guchūreki|qq=}} and {{ill|Kana Calendars|lt=Kana Calendars|lt=Kanareki|ja|仮名暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanareki|qq=}}, catered to specific audiences. Between the {{ill|Jōkyō|en|Jōkyō|qid=Q1146221|lt=Jōkyō era|1=Jōkyō}} and the Genroku era, Zassho expanded to cover various topics, including daily life and romantic relationships, marking a peak in publication during the {{ill|Bakumatsu|en|Bakumatsu|qid=Q804558|lt=Bakumatsu period|1=Bakumatsu}}, with over 100 types in circulation. By this time, Zassho evolved beyond calendrical and divinatory purposes, serving a more encyclopedic role.
A reprinted version of the *Tenpō New Selected Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei* is available.
== References == * Yoshiro Okada, "Zassho," in *Kokushi Daijiten 6*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985, ISBN 978-4-642-00506-7. * Japanese History Dictionary Editorial Committee, ed., *Nihonshi Kōjiten*, Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1997, ISBN 978-4-634-62010-0. * Takahisa Miyauchi, "Zassho," in *Nihon Minzoku Daijiten: Volume 1*, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2000, ISBN 978-4-642-01332-1. * Jingūkan, *Eitai Ōzassho Manreki Taisei*, Jingūkan, 1998, ISBN 978-4-915-26122-0.
